title: For British Eyes Only

jokes.text: I don't even have a girl, much less a stupid one.
jokes.setup: Michael misunderstands 'stupid girl problem' to mean the girl is stupid; George Michael corrects him saying 'the problem is stupid, the girl isn't stupid'
jokes.punchline: George Michael then reveals he doesn't even have a girl — all of this anxiety over nothing
jokes.explanation: The three-beat structure — the problem is stupid / the girl isn't / he doesn't even have a girl — is elegantly compact. George Michael's lovelorn nerdiness is perfectly captured. Rewatch bonus because the narrator's interpolation ('because the girl was his cousin Maeby') makes this immediately darker and funnier in retrospect.

jokes.text: Narrator reveals the girl is his cousin Maeby, immediately after George Michael insists the girl isn't stupid.
jokes.setup: George Michael defensively clarifies the girl isn't stupid
jokes.punchline: Narrator cuts in to reveal the girl is his cousin, implying a deeply problematic crush
jokes.explanation: The narrator's deadpan interpolation cuts in precisely at George Michael's most earnest moment of defense, creating a perfect ironic reversal. The joke weaponizes the show's unique narrator device to maximum effect. This kind of narrator-as-punchline is signature AD craft.

jokes.text: Michael awoke to find his camping trip going south.
jokes.setup: Standard 'previously on' recap language sets up a neutral plot description
jokes.punchline: 'Going south' is both literal (George Sr. fled) and idiomatic (everything went wrong)
jokes.explanation: A competent narrator double-meaning gag, efficient but not exceptional. AD does these constantly; this one earns a modest chuckle.

jokes.text: Oh, dear God. Send me back to prison.
jokes.setup: George Sr. pleaded with Michael not to send him back to prison so he could be with his 'dear love Lucille'
jokes.punchline: One look at Lucille as his 'warden' and he immediately begs to go back to prison
jokes.explanation: The speed of the reversal is what makes it land hard. We barely finish the setup (Lucille as warden) before he's already recanting. Perfect comedic economy. George Sr.'s terror of Lucille is deeply character-specific.

jokes.text: You gotta send me back to prison. / More touching.
jokes.setup: George Sr. desperately says 'You gotta send me back to prison'
jokes.punchline: The narrator dryly comments 'More touching' — describing this as a tender family moment
jokes.explanation: The narrator's sarcastic 'More touching' is a perfect deadpan undercut. AD's narrator is at its best when functioning as ironic Greek chorus, and this is a clean example.

jokes.text: Buster describes feeling like the Invisible Man: 'Hello! Can anyone see me?' — while physically missing his hand.
jokes.setup: Buster complains no one notices him since Father returned
jokes.punchline: The irony of the one-handed man calling himself 'invisible' and waving to be seen — the hand he's waving with is a rubber prosthetic
jokes.explanation: Buster's missing hand gives his 'invisible' complaint a literal visual irony that only works because of the show's ongoing storyline. Character-specific and layered.

jokes.text: Michael suggests Buster get a girlfriend, then immediately pivots: '...A pet. How about a turtle?'
jokes.setup: Michael starts to suggest Buster get 'a girlf—' clearly heading to 'girlfriend'
jokes.punchline: He catches himself and pivots to 'a pet... how about a turtle?' — implying getting a girlfriend for Buster is too preposterous to finish the thought
jokes.explanation: The mid-word self-correction is well-timed and character-specific — Michael's exasperated brotherly instinct to protect Buster from himself. The turtle as consolation prize lands because it's so random yet delivered so earnestly.

jokes.text: I've always loved those leathery little snappy faces. / You certainly have a type.
jokes.setup: Buster says he's always loved turtles' 'leathery little snappy faces'
jokes.punchline: Lucille shoots back 'You certainly have a type' — implying Buster is attracted to his aged, domineering mother
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional AD writing. In five words, Lucille simultaneously insults herself, her son, and the entire Bluth family dynamic. The callback to Buster's history with Lucille 2 (another older woman) makes it devastatingly specific. 'You certainly have a type' could only be said by this character in this show. Deserves its 8.5.

jokes.text: Can't you keep my hand to yourself? / 'Used it for something'?
jokes.setup: Buster reacts with 'Can't you keep my hand to yourself?' — outrage at his parents using his prosthetic
jokes.punchline: Michael's incredulous echo of 'used it for something?' is the straight-man reaction beat that confirms the audience's worst interpretation
jokes.explanation: The double-beat — Buster's outrage followed by Michael's confused disgust — milks the previous joke effectively. Michael's reaction confirms the sexual reading for anyone who missed it. Functional escalation of id 9.

jokes.text: My breakfast? / My friskiness. / Mama horny, Michael.
jokes.setup: Lucille teases 'guess what else is back' after George Sr.'s return; Michael hopefully guesses 'My breakfast?'
jokes.punchline: Lucille announces 'My friskiness' and then bluntly tells Michael 'Mama horny, Michael'
jokes.explanation: 'Mama horny, Michael' is one of those perfectly quotable, deeply wrong lines that only Arrested Development would give to a 60-something matriarch. The three-beat structure (guess / reveal / blunt statement) is airtight. High quotability because the line is so memorably awful.

jokes.text: I'm amazed Dad hasn't strangled himself with his belt yet. / Oh, we're into all kinds of freaky stuff.
jokes.setup: Michael says sarcastically he's amazed George hasn't strangled himself — meaning out of frustration with Lucille
jokes.punchline: Lucille takes it sexually: 'We're into all kinds of freaky stuff'
jokes.explanation: The double-meaning of 'strangle himself with his belt' — frustration vs. auto-erotic asphyxiation — is perfectly exploited by Lucille's response. The joke weaponizes the gap between Michael's exasperated tone and Lucille's cheerful sexual revelation. Escalation of the 'parents are sexually active and horrible' thread.

jokes.text: Why do I eat breakfast before I come here?
jokes.setup: Michael has just heard about his parents' sex life and the rubber hand
jokes.punchline: His muttered self-questioning implies he's been made sick enough to lose his appetite — a perfectly understated disgust
jokes.explanation: Clean economy. One line captures Michael's perpetual exasperation with his family without over-explaining. The humor is in the resignation — he knows this happens every time and still hasn't learned.

jokes.text: Gob's 'Free Bird' illusion description: a magical bird — 'me in a beak' — stands on a platform, puff of smoke, then high above in an empty cage...
jokes.setup: Gob excitedly describes his grand illusion for their father's court appearance
jokes.punchline: The illusion is comically elaborate, self-serious, and conceptually vague — 'me in a beak' and 'once-empty cage' create an absurd image
jokes.explanation: Gob's delusional self-importance is perfectly captured. The illusion is described with the grandiosity of a master magician but reveals itself to be conceptually silly. Rewatch bonus because the illusion's later evolution (from Free Bird to Free Chicken) pays this off.

jokes.text: A guy that kind of looks like you? / No. Not how I do the trick. / Even if it was, how'd the look-alike get up there? He was hiding behind this mirror here?
jokes.setup: Michael casually guesses how the cage trick works
jokes.punchline: Gob indignantly denies it — but Michael has accidentally described exactly how it works, and Gob's outrage only confirms it
jokes.explanation: Michael accidentally reverse-engineers the trick while Gob desperately denies it — the comedy is in Gob's incompetent magician pride being punctured by his oblivious brother. Rewatch bonus because we later see the trick actually uses a look-alike hiding in the trick.

jokes.text: A 'protest-acular.'
jokes.setup: Gob describes his courtroom illusion as both a protest and a spectacular
jokes.punchline: He portmanteaus them into 'protest-acular' with complete sincerity
jokes.explanation: A tight wordplay invention that perfectly captures Gob's theatrical delusion. The word itself is funny; the sincerity of its deployment makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Excuse me. I was just looking for a marker... A Magic Marker.
jokes.setup: Tobias enters the scene apparently overhearing the magic trick planning and wanting to participate
jokes.punchline: His excuse for being there — 'looking for a Magic Marker' — is transparently false and painfully see-through, yet he says it with complete earnestness
jokes.explanation: Tobias's desperation to be included and his terrible cover stories are core character comedy. Not the sharpest Tobias joke but functional.

jokes.text: I want it out of my living room. Put it in Buster's room. That's where she keeps things she has no need for.
jokes.setup: Lucille wants the illusion apparatus removed from the living room
jokes.punchline: She specifies Buster's room as the dumping ground for useless things — Buster himself is in the room
jokes.explanation: Lucille's casual cruelty toward Buster is a hallmark of the show. Calling his room where she puts 'things she has no need for' — with Buster presumably present — is devastating maternal indifference delivered without a second thought. Deeply character-specific.

jokes.text: Gob ignoring his son Steve Holt: 'Well, then just tell him that Steve Holt stopped by, okay?'
jokes.setup: Someone apparently relays that Steve Holt is there to see Gob (his biological son)
jokes.punchline: Gob responds as if Steve Holt is just some acquaintance to be brushed off — not his own son
jokes.explanation: The absurdity of Gob telling someone to tell Steve Holt that 'Steve Holt stopped by' — referring to his own son in the third person as if taking a message — is a perfect distillation of Gob's parental denial. Rewatch bonus when you know Steve Holt's arc.

jokes.text: He'd been ducking him ever since. / Tom? Come on over here. I've got a vitamin for you. / He's the one who can't see me. / Because you were hiding in the trick? / Illusion, Michael.
jokes.setup: The narrator reveals Gob has been hiding from Steve Holt; a boy (Tom) is called over with the offer of 'a vitamin'
jokes.punchline: It's revealed Gob has been hiding inside his own magic trick to avoid his son; Michael asks 'because you were hiding in the trick?' and Gob corrects him with the running gag: 'Illusion, Michael'
jokes.explanation: The 'trick/illusion' correction is one of the show's most beloved running gags, and its deployment here is earned: Gob is simultaneously avoiding his son, hiding in a magic apparatus, and still prioritizing semantic correctness. Layered character comedy.

jokes.text: I so very much would like to be in your 'prostate-icular' as your look-alike.
jokes.setup: Tobias wants to be involved in Gob's 'protest-acular' illusion as a look-alike
jokes.punchline: Tobias mispronounces 'protest-acular' as 'prostate-icular' — an unintentional sexual/anatomical pun entirely in keeping with Tobias's oblivious double-entendre personality
jokes.explanation: This is peak Tobias. 'Prostate-icular' takes the earlier 'protest-acular' wordplay and immediately tops it by filtering it through Tobias's unique inability to say anything without making it anatomically or sexually suspect. Character integration is near-perfect — this joke literally could not be made by any other character in any other show. A genuine standout.

jokes.text: They're gonna see the ink. It's more of a leading man role. But it's not really done that way. Guess I shouldn't have used a permanent marker.
jokes.setup: Gob rejects Tobias as a look-alike because he's visibly different; Tobias has apparently already drawn a face on himself with a marker to look like Gob
jokes.punchline: The punchline arrives: 'Guess I shouldn't have used a permanent marker' — Tobias has permanently marked his own face in his desperation to get the role
jokes.explanation: The 'Magic Marker' planted earlier pays off perfectly. Tobias used it to draw Gob's likeness on himself — a desperate, permanent, and self-defeating act. The callback rewards attentive viewers and lands as a visual gag we're inferring from context.

jokes.text: Starting to see why you want that divorce.
jokes.setup: Michael witnesses Tobias's permanently-marked face and general behavior
jokes.punchline: Michael's dry assessment — 'starting to see why you want that divorce' — acknowledges the absurdity without over-explaining it
jokes.explanation: Clean Michael straight-man reaction. Gets its chuckle from economy and timing but doesn't add much beyond confirming what we're seeing. Functional.

jokes.text: Instead of spending money on a divorce — maybe I should get something faster. / Not the car. I said no to the Volvo. / The Lexus. The Volvo didn't have satellite radio, which is crazy. I mean, what are we, Amish?
jokes.setup: Lindsay agrees divorce is expensive and considers something 'faster'
jokes.punchline: It's a car — she has completely lost the thread of discussing her marriage and pivoted to complaining about not having a Lexus with satellite radio
jokes.explanation: Lindsay's vapid materialism — pivoting from divorce to car complaints mid-sentence — is perfectly in character. 'What are we, Amish?' as a response to no satellite radio is a great character-comedy beat. Her priorities are entirely skewed and the joke trusts us to see it.

jokes.text: Lot of that was the coma. / Yeah. I've heard your side of it.
jokes.setup: Michael points out Tobias and Lindsay 'weren't even speaking toward the end'
jokes.punchline: Tobias explains 'lot of that was the coma' — as if being in a coma is a legitimate excuse that Michael is dismissing by saying 'I've heard your side of it'
jokes.explanation: The coma as a 'side of the story' is gloriously absurd. Michael's weary 'I've heard your side of it' treats an objective medical fact as a contested marital grievance. Both characters are wrong in perfectly funny ways. Sharp economy.

jokes.text: Look who finally got his pants on. / You've got 10 minutes.
jokes.setup: George Sr. enters the room, apparently having taken a while to get dressed
jokes.punchline: Lucille's backhanded acknowledgment — 'look who finally got his pants on' — treats a grown man's basic self-care as an achievement worth noting, then immediately demeans him with a time limit
jokes.explanation: A nice character beat establishing Lucille's dominance over George Sr. under house arrest. Nothing exceptional but lands reliably.

jokes.text: You're the one that said no to prison. / I was wrong. There you just had to shut your eyes and take it. Here you have to shut your eyes and give it.
jokes.setup: Michael notes George chose house arrest over prison
jokes.punchline: George's distinction between prison ('shut your eyes and take it') and living with Lucille ('shut your eyes and give it') implies both are sexual violations — being with Lucille is worse because it requires active participation
jokes.explanation: This is genuinely exceptional writing. The parallel structure ('take it' / 'give it') is elegant and the implication — that consensual sex with his wife is more traumatic than prison rape — is delivered with George's absolute, world-weary sincerity. Dark, precise, and perfectly constructed. Earns its 8.3.

jokes.text: We need someone big behind us. Our own private Matlock. / I got him. / Got who? / Andy Griffith.
jokes.setup: George says they need 'a private Matlock'
jokes.punchline: He has literally hired Andy Griffith — the actor who played Matlock — not an actual attorney
jokes.explanation: The reveal is excellently timed — George's confident 'I got him' followed by Michael's perfectly placed 'Got who?' setting up the name drop. The joke's escalation (pricing tiers) is where it really takes off — see id 29.

jokes.text: For 10 grand, he'll sit behind us and read the paper. For 15, he'll sit at the defense table. For $20,000, he'll twice lean forward and whisper something in your ear. White suit — that's extra.
jokes.setup: George Sr. has hired Andy Griffith (the actor) to appear at his trial
jokes.punchline: The meticulously tiered pricing for increasingly minimal gestures — culminating in the white suit as a premium add-on — escalates to absurdist perfection
jokes.explanation: This is a masterclass in escalation comedy. Each tier is funnier than the last: sitting behind them reading a paper → sitting at the defense table → leaning forward twice to whisper → white suit is extra. The specificity (twice, forward, whisper) makes it sublime. 'White suit — that's extra' as a throwaway coda is perfect. One of the episode's best jokes.

jokes.text: Oh. White suit... That's extra.
jokes.setup: The Andy Griffith price structure has already established three tiers
jokes.punchline: George Sr. adds a fourth item almost as an afterthought — the white suit (Matlock's signature look) costs extra — implying there's a detailed contract for a TV actor's courtroom cosplay
jokes.explanation: The topper on the Andy Griffith bit. 'That's extra' as a three-word button on an escalating comedy structure is flawless. The pause implied before the line (George Sr. checking his mental notes) and the casual 'Oh' starter make it feel like a genuine afterthought — which is the delivery style that makes toppers work. Four words, maximum laugh.

jokes.text: Andy Griffith wasn't the bombshell?
jokes.setup: George says 'ready for the bombshell?' after already revealing the Andy Griffith plan
jokes.punchline: Michael's flat 'Andy Griffith wasn't the bombshell?' perfectly captures his disbelief that something even more absurd is coming
jokes.explanation: A perfect straight-man beat. The brevity is the whole joke — Michael's incredulity that the Griffith revelation wasn't the peak absurdity. Economy 9.0 is justified.

jokes.text: I'm a patsy. I was set up — by the Brits. / Don't call it that.
jokes.setup: George Sr. says the British builders were 'operating outside the OC'
jokes.punchline: Michael immediately interrupts: 'Don't call it that' — the 'OC' correction is a running gag about the show literally being set in Orange County during The O.C.'s peak popularity
jokes.explanation: A meta gag with layers — referencing the actual show The O.C. which AD competed against, delivered as a character quirk. Gets funnier the more you know about the show's production context. Clean and fast.

jokes.text: We've got a picture of you with Saddam Hussein. / I thought that was the guy who played the Soup Nazi.
jokes.setup: Michael confronts George Sr. with a photo of him with Saddam Hussein
jokes.punchline: George mistook Saddam Hussein for the actor who played the Soup Nazi on Seinfeld — then told him how much he liked his work
jokes.explanation: The precision of this joke is extraordinary. It's not just 'I thought he was someone else' — it's specifically the Soup Nazi actor, and he complimented him on his work. The layers: (1) confusing Saddam with a TV character, (2) the specific character choice (a famously shouting Middle Eastern-coded character), (3) telling him he liked his 'work.' Dark, smart, and very funny.

jokes.text: Not in every case. [re: a photo with Saddam ending your career]
jokes.setup: George Sr. says 'A picture like that, Michael, can end your career'
jokes.punchline: Michael's flat 'Not in every case' — implying he knows of careers that survived Saddam photos, which is itself absurd
jokes.explanation: Efficient deadpan. 'Not in every case' as a response to 'a Saddam photo ends your career' is a nice absurdist non-sequitur from Michael — implying a world where this is more routine than it should be.

jokes.text: Oh, they're polite and the men all sound gay, but they will rip out your heart and — their breath —
jokes.setup: George warns that British people are dangerous
jokes.punchline: His warning escalates from ominous ('rip out your heart') to petty ('their breath') — deflating his own threat assessment with a trivial complaint
jokes.explanation: The anti-climax (pivoting from 'rip out your heart' to 'their breath') is a decent misdirection but the homophobic aside and the breath gag are fairly dated. The structure works but the content is weak.

jokes.text: Why does everybody think that I'm scared of girls? / Because you're a chicken. You're a chicken.
jokes.setup: Michael defends himself against the accusation of being scared of women
jokes.punchline: George does the chicken dance/call, establishing the running gag of the Bluth family chicken imitations
jokes.explanation: The chicken imitation gag is one of AD's most beloved running bits. This particular instance sets up a multi-character pay-off later in the episode. The rewatch bonus is high because each family member's different (terrible) chicken impression is a source of ongoing delight.

jokes.text: Has anyone in this family ever even seen a chicken?
jokes.setup: Multiple family members perform chicken impressions that apparently bear no resemblance to actual chickens
jokes.punchline: Michael's exasperated question implies all of their chicken impressions are completely wrong — he's the only one noticing the absurdity
jokes.explanation: Seven words that perfectly encapsulate a running gag while adding a meta-comic layer: the implication that ALL of them do it wrong, that none of them have apparently ever seen a chicken, and that Michael is the only sane man. Highly quotable, rewatch gold. Earns its 8.0.

jokes.text: Lindsay, I did get you a car. / Is it the Lexus? / It's more like a Lincoln. / Oh, come on! There's no satellite radio, but there's a banjo in the closet. And watch out for live-ins.
jokes.setup: Lindsay was hoping for a Lexus with satellite radio
jokes.punchline: She gets a Lincoln (a hearse or old car implied) — with a banjo in the closet and apparent squatters ('live-ins')
jokes.explanation: The callback to the satellite radio complaint lands well, and 'watch out for live-ins' as a completely offhand additional warning is perfectly delivered. The escalating horror of the car (no sat radio → banjo → actual people living in it) is nicely structured.

jokes.text: Lanes reverse! / You're driving on the wrong side, you bloomin' idiot!
jokes.setup: Michael arrives at 'Wee Britain' — the English section of Orange County
jokes.punchline: Even in an American suburb, the British enclave insists on driving on the left side of the road
jokes.explanation: A functional world-building joke establishing the absurdity of 'Wee Britain' — a British enclave that insists on British driving rules in Orange County. Not deep but earns its laughs from commitment to the bit.

jokes.text: They're for British eyes only.
jokes.setup: Michael asks to check building business records; the clerk refuses without a UK passport
jokes.punchline: 'For British eyes only' — a riff on 'For Your Eyes Only' (James Bond) that also serves as a plot device and episode title
jokes.explanation: The episode's title joke, delivered with complete bureaucratic straightness. Works as wordplay, as plot exposition, and as a Bond reference. The rewatch bonus: knowing this is the episode title makes it land differently.

jokes.text: Don't I look kind of British? / Perhaps if you're willing to lose 20 pounds.
jokes.setup: Michael tries to pass as British to access the files
jokes.punchline: The clerk implies Michael could look British if he lost 20 pounds — a backhanded national stereotype (British people are thinner/more gaunt)
jokes.explanation: A decent burn delivered with British condescension. Not the sharpest joke in the episode but gets its chuckle from the clerk's absolute sincerity.

jokes.text: The clerk was asking for a bribe — but this was lost on Michael. / You guys do go for the jugular.
jokes.setup: The narrator explains the clerk is suggesting Michael lose weight as a way of asking for a bribe (thin = money = more attractive/powerful)
jokes.punchline: Michael completely misreads it as a personal insult about his weight and responds with wounded admiration: 'You guys do go for the jugular'
jokes.explanation: The narrator's explanatory function becomes the joke — the audience needs the narrator to explain the miscommunication, and Michael's response ('go for the jugular') is so completely wrong that it's its own punchline. Layered AD comedy at work.

jokes.text: What's the soup of the day, Mum? / Bread. / And the soup of the day is bread.
jokes.setup: The pub barman asks his mother what the soup of the day is
jokes.punchline: The soup of the day is bread — not a soup, just bread — delivered with complete non-ironic sincerity
jokes.explanation: Beautifully absurdist. The triple structure (question / answer / formal announcement of wrong answer) is tight. Bread-as-soup is a perfect non-sequitur that commits completely to its own internal logic. A delightful aside.

jokes.text: Not her. / Wait for it. [Visual: Michael spots Rita]
jokes.setup: The narrator describes Michael seeing 'the most beautiful woman he'd ever seen' as the camera likely lands on someone comedically wrong
jokes.punchline: The narrator corrects it — 'not her / wait for it' — and then reveals the actual beautiful woman; the narrator is performing comedic misdirection in real time
jokes.explanation: The narrator actively participating in a visual gag — misdirecting the audience to the wrong woman before the reveal — is a genuinely clever meta-structural joke. It uses the show's unique narrator device to control the audience's gaze. Earns its 8.0 through formal innovation.

jokes.text: I will pay you. / You'll pay me? / Not for sex. You're gonna think I'm Jack the Ripper, right? / Didn't he kill prostitutes or... / I'm not a prostitute. / Then I shall let you live.
jokes.setup: Michael asks Rita to accompany him and awkwardly mentions he'll pay her — she reasonably assumes prostitution
jokes.punchline: Michael invokes Jack the Ripper in trying to clarify, Rita corrects that Ripper killed prostitutes, Michael confirms she's safe since she's not one — each beat makes it worse
jokes.explanation: A perfectly constructed cringe escalation. Each attempt Michael makes to fix the situation makes it worse: paying → sex → Ripper → prostitutes → 'then I shall let you live.' The final line from Rita ('I shall let you live') is darkly playful and suggests she's game for this bizarre exchange. Michael's incompetence with women is brilliantly showcased. Rewatch bonus when you understand Rita's secret.

jokes.text: This is my worst hello. Let me try again.
jokes.setup: Michael's introduction to Rita has gone catastrophically: paid-for-sex implication, Jack the Ripper reference
jokes.punchline: Michael's calm self-assessment ('worst hello') and offer to retry is both an understatement and a signal that he's unaware how far beyond 'hello' they've gotten
jokes.explanation: Michael's self-aware embarrassment is a reliable comedic mode for him. 'My worst hello' as a category (implying he has a ranked list of hellos) is a funny frame. Works well as a scene reset.

jokes.text: Well, lucky for you, my whole face is British.
jokes.setup: Michael explains he needs access to files that are 'for British eyes only'
jokes.punchline: Rita offers that her 'whole face is British' — a charming, slightly odd construction that suggests she's not just offering her eyes but her entire face
jokes.explanation: A nice callback to the episode title joke. 'My whole face is British' is a sweetly odd phrasing that gains extra resonance on rewatch given Rita's secret (her innocence/mental age).

jokes.text: I thought you said you were open till 8:00. / Oh, yes — GMT. Everything here is on Greenwich mean time.
jokes.setup: Michael arrives at the records office with Rita only to find it's closed
jokes.punchline: The clerk reveals everything in Wee Britain runs on GMT — meaning they're 8 hours off from California time — complete bureaucratic commitment to the bit
jokes.explanation: The GMT gag is a strong world-building joke. The idea that this Orange County neighborhood runs its businesses on London time is the kind of absurdist commitment that makes Wee Britain a great recurring location.

jokes.text: I like to think they teach me. / Like what? How to eat crayons, throw tantrums... / Oh, no. Not when I'm there. I'm very good.
jokes.setup: Rita says she teaches at a school called Slowbrooke and adds 'I like to think they teach me'
jokes.punchline: Michael sarcastically asks what the kids teach her — eating crayons, throwing tantrums — and Rita earnestly clarifies she doesn't do those things when she's there
jokes.explanation: On first watch this seems like Rita correcting Michael's sarcasm. On rewatch, knowing Rita's MRF secret, her clarification that she doesn't eat crayons or throw tantrums 'when she's there' reads entirely differently — as though these are things she might do. High rewatch bonus.

jokes.text: It's turning into one of my worst good-byes. / Farewell, and, uh... our paths shan't cross again. / As you like it.
jokes.setup: Michael tries to say goodbye to Rita after their awkward interaction
jokes.punchline: He delivers the theatrical 'our paths shan't cross again' as a goodbye, and Rita responds with the equally theatrical 'as you like it' — both treating a casual farewell as a period drama
jokes.explanation: Michael's self-aware 'worst good-byes' comment followed by immediately making it worse is good character work. Rita's 'as you like it' — echoing Shakespeare while being completely literal — is charming and rewatch-coded.

jokes.text: A Mary Poppins performer hits a tourist; Michael and Rita laugh, then Michael says 'I'm sorry, it's wrong to laugh' — and immediately uses it as an excuse to stay and ask her out.
jokes.setup: An unlicensed Mary Poppins street performer causes some kind of incident (later described as 'takes down a tourist')
jokes.punchline: Michael uses their shared laughter over someone being hurt as the social bonding moment that gives him the confidence to re-approach Rita
jokes.explanation: The Poppins incident works on multiple levels — as slapstick setup, as the catalyst for Michael's date, and as a world detail (unlicensed Poppins performer as a hazard in Wee Britain). Michael's guilty 'wrong to laugh' pivot into using it to ask her out is a nice character beat.

jokes.text: I suppose I could sneak out at nap time.
jokes.setup: Michael asks if Rita can meet him tomorrow during her school day
jokes.punchline: Rita, a teacher, says she'll 'sneak out at nap time' — a phrase that implies she participates in the nap rather than supervising it
jokes.explanation: Another Rita joke that lands differently on rewatch. A teacher 'sneaking out at nap time' is mildly amusing; knowing that Rita herself naps with the children makes it significantly funnier in retrospect.

jokes.text: And I shall drop you off alive, hooker or no.
jokes.setup: Michael's terrible first impression (Jack the Ripper, possible prostitution implication)
jokes.punchline: He tries to close the conversation reassuringly with the explicit promise to keep her alive 'hooker or no' — a callback to both the prostitution misunderstanding AND the Jack the Ripper tangent
jokes.explanation: A pitch-perfect callback button on the introductory scene. The phrase 'hooker or no' — treating her professional status as an afterthought but still an open question — is brilliantly specific. Michael thinking this is a good parting line while clearly making everything worse is peak character comedy. 'Shall' returns as Michael's faux-archaic register. Highly quotable and well-structured.

jokes.text: And I shall drop you off alive, hooker or no.
jokes.setup: Michael and Rita are saying their goodbyes after arranging a date
jokes.punchline: Michael circles back to the Jack the Ripper/prostitute misunderstanding with 'I shall drop you off alive, hooker or no' — reassuring her of her safety in Ripper terms
jokes.explanation: A perfect callback that takes the earlier cringe joke and turns it into a genuine charm moment. The absurdity of 'hooker or no' as a casual reassurance, delivered with sincerity, is a great character beat. Highly quotable — 'I shall drop you off alive, hooker or no' is the kind of line AD does better than anyone.

jokes.text: Oh, look who's back. / Are you gonna buy this time, or you just curious?
jokes.setup: Tobias returns to the costume shop he'd 'stumbled upon years earlier'
jokes.punchline: The owner's 'are you gonna buy this time, or you just curious?' implies Tobias has made a habit of visiting the costume shop without purchasing — the owner recognizes him
jokes.explanation: Sets up Tobias's relationship with the costume shop — a recurring AD location associated with his acting aspirations and closeted life. The owner's weariness implies this is a well-established pattern. Competent setup joke.

jokes.text: I suppose I'm, uh, buy-curious.
jokes.setup: The costume shop owner asks Tobias if he's going to buy something or just browse again
jokes.punchline: Tobias responds 'I suppose I'm buy-curious' — an accidental (or not) portmanteau of 'buying' and 'bi-curious' that adds to Tobias's ongoing accidental sexual innuendo about his own sexuality
jokes.explanation: This is one of the great Tobias lines. 'Buy-curious' is a perfect portmanteau: it functions as a literal answer ('I'm curious about buying'), as 'bi-curious' (questioning his sexuality), and as a callback to his previous visits to this shop (which the shopkeeper references). Three meanings, four words, delivered with complete obliviousness. This is the high-water mark of AD's Tobias double-entendre craft and earns its near-9 score.

jokes.text: I have a big TV opportunity. / This is where all the big TVs come.
jokes.setup: Tobias says he has a 'big TV opportunity'
jokes.punchline: The shop owner hears 'big TVs' (large television sets) and responds that yes, this is where they come — a complete miscommunication about the word 'TV'
jokes.explanation: A quick wordplay miscommunication that works well. 'This is where all the big TVs come' as a response to 'TV opportunity' — treating television appearances and television sets as the same word — is a tight little gag.

jokes.text: Let's start with the Eve Arden.
jokes.setup: Tobias is at a costume shop, ostensibly to prepare for Gob's illusion
jokes.punchline: He immediately asks for a specific theatrical costume type named 'Eve Arden' — a 1940s actress known for playing acerbic supporting women — suggesting Tobias has a very specific and niche theatrical costume vocabulary
jokes.explanation: A background joke for people who know who Eve Arden is. Adds to Tobias's theatrical pretension but the laugh depends on obscure knowledge. Moderate craft, modest impact.

jokes.text: I wanted to tell you that I got myself a date with a beautiful woman named Rita, and you're the chicken, not me. / Cluck, cluck, cluck, cluck, cluck...
jokes.setup: Michael calls his sister specifically to announce he has a date and to flip the 'chicken' accusation back on her
jokes.punchline: Lindsay responds with her own chicken impression (cluck, cluck, cluck) — a new variant of the family chicken gag
jokes.explanation: The running gag pays off with another family member's chicken impression. The setup (Michael calling specifically to brag and call Lindsay a chicken) is good character work — his pettiness with his sister is endearing.

jokes.text: Okay. That's a pretty good chicken.
jokes.setup: Lindsay does a chicken impression in retaliation
jokes.punchline: Michael, after demanding to know if anyone in the family has seen a chicken, is forced to admit Lindsay's version is pretty good
jokes.explanation: The perfect callback payoff. Michael explicitly questioned whether anyone in the family had seen a chicken — now he has to reluctantly concede that Lindsay's impression is 'pretty good.' The defeat in those five words, and what it implies about the others' impressions, is gold. Earns its 8.0 through elegant callback structure.

jokes.text: Wow. Dad was not kidding about their breath.
jokes.setup: Michael has just been aggressively threatened by a British man with foul language
jokes.punchline: His only comment is on the man's breath — the trivial detail George Sr. mentioned earlier when warning about British people
jokes.explanation: A near-perfect callback. Michael was just physically threatened and his only takeaway is confirming his father's complaint about British breath. It's simultaneously an absurd deflation of the threat and a character moment — Michael's crisis management is focused on the least important detail. Sharp economy and structure.

jokes.text: I know what an erection feels like, Michael. No. It's the opposite. It's like my heart is getting hard.
jokes.setup: Michael asks if Gob's strange feeling about Steve Holt might be love
jokes.punchline: Gob asserts he knows what an erection feels like (this is not that), then describes his heart 'getting hard' — which is both literally what hearts do (harden with emotion) and an inadvertent sexual metaphor that undercuts his entire clarification
jokes.explanation: A genuine standout. 'I know what an erection feels like, Michael' is one of those AD lines that works on pure character specificity — only Gob would clarify this so unnecessarily and precisely. Then 'it's like my heart is getting hard' immediately reproduces the thing he just denied, making it a double entendre and an accidental self-own in one. High quotability, high impact, strong craft.

jokes.text: You know, the feeling that you're feeling, it's just what many of us call a feeling.
jokes.setup: Gob is overwhelmed by his emotions and Michael tries to explain them
jokes.punchline: Michael's circular, tautological explanation — 'what many of us call a feeling' — is deliberately unhelpful and reveals Michael's own emotional limitations
jokes.explanation: The profound uselessness of Michael's emotional coaching — 'what many of us call a feeling' — is both a Gob joke (he doesn't recognize basic emotions) and a Michael joke (his advice is circular). A layered character moment delivered with great economy.

jokes.text: Hey, Dad? / Whoa! / It's my son, Gob.
jokes.setup: Gob has just been discussing his fatherly feelings; Steve Holt appears
jokes.punchline: Gob's terrified 'Whoa!' at the arrival of his own son — then Michael calmly identifying him — shows Gob is more frightened than moved
jokes.explanation: The immediate deflation of Gob's emotional breakthrough — he was just talking about being ready to be a father and then shouts 'Whoa!' in fear at his actual son — is a perfect structure of setup and payoff within the same scene.

jokes.text: You know, and what is it with the fathers in this family always trying to deceive their... [Michael realizes George hired the British threatening man]
jokes.setup: Michael starts to make a general observation about fathers deceiving their children
jokes.punchline: He interrupts himself with the realization that George Sr. must have hired the threatening British man — his generic family complaint becomes a specific plot deduction
jokes.explanation: A decent structural beat where Michael's self-interruption does double duty — showing his thought process and providing comedy through the truncated generalization about his family. Functional but not outstanding.

jokes.text: And that's when Michael realized that the man who threatened him must have been hired by George Sr.
jokes.setup: Gob's accidental arrival of Steve Holt (who surprised Gob despite Gob hiding) mirrors Michael's realization
jokes.punchline: The narrator uses Gob hiding from his son as the logic trigger for Michael realizing his father hired someone to scare him — visual parallel of fathers hiding from their problems leading to a story revelation
jokes.explanation: The structural joke is that Michael's insight comes from watching Gob's cowardice, not from any actual evidence. It's a nice narrative comedy beat but more plot-functional than purely comedic.

jokes.text: That's a woman's wig. / I was told it was a bob.
jokes.setup: George Michael is wearing a wig, apparently for the magic trick look-alike role
jokes.punchline: 'I was told it was a bob' — the name for the hairstyle — but Bob is also a man's name, suggesting George Michael was misled about the gender of the wig style
jokes.explanation: 'I was told it was a bob' works as both a misunderstanding of the hairstyle name and a suggestion that someone lied to George Michael about what a bob is. Clean and economical.

jokes.text: It's a wonderful performance. You're a regular Brad Garrett.
jokes.setup: Michael is convinced George Sr.'s shock at the British threat is fake
jokes.punchline: He calls him 'a regular Brad Garrett' — a dig at Brad Garrett's supporting actor Emmy wins for Everybody Loves Raymond, suggesting George's performance is Emmy-caliber supporting work
jokes.explanation: A pop culture reference that works if you know Brad Garrett's Emmy reputation but doesn't add much beyond that. Competent but not deep.

jokes.text: But I don't watch TV at 3:00 in the afternoon...
jokes.setup: Michael sarcastically says George is 'a regular Brad Garrett'
jokes.punchline: He adds he doesn't watch TV at 3pm — a self-aware dig implying that's when Everybody Loves Raymond reruns air, and that he's above that; also a meta-joke about AD airing in a different timeslot
jokes.explanation: A minor meta aside. Works as a snob joke and potentially as a meta-comment on TV scheduling. Not a strong laugh on its own.

jokes.text: But I don't watch TV at 3:00 in the afternoon.
jokes.setup: Michael compares George Sr. to a TV actor
jokes.punchline: He then distances himself from the reference by noting he doesn't watch TV at 3 PM — implying Brad Garrett is daytime TV fare, a status insult within a status insult
jokes.explanation: Extension of the Brad Garrett joke that adds a class/status layer. The timing implication (daytime TV is beneath Michael) is a minor escalation. Competent but still riding the Brad Garrett setup which wasn't that strong.

jokes.text: He said he was gonna come after somebody stupid in our family. / That could be Buster. / Could be Gob.
jokes.setup: The threatening man said he'd target 'someone stupid' in the Bluth family
jokes.punchline: Both Michael and George Sr. immediately identify multiple candidates — 'That could be Buster' / 'Could be Gob' — the rapid agreement implying the family's vulnerability is well-established
jokes.explanation: The rhythm of the exchange is what makes this work — each man raising a candidate and the other nodding along. The fact that they're having this conversation seriously (not as a joke) while identifying vulnerable family members is darkly funny family dynamics comedy.

jokes.text: He said he was gonna come after somebody stupid in our family. / That could be Buster. / Could be Gob.
jokes.setup: Michael and George Sr. realize the threat is real and a family member might be targeted
jokes.punchline: They immediately and simultaneously agree that Buster or Gob are the obvious targets — the comedy being that neither considers this cruel, just accurate
jokes.explanation: The casual cruelty of Michael and George immediately nominating Buster and Gob as 'the stupid ones' without hesitation is great family-dysfunction comedy. Their synchronicity implies they've had this calculus before.

jokes.text: Sorry, I just wanted to let you know that I'm not gonna be needing your passport after all... So it's bye-bye to Rita, then? / Yes, I'll shake your hand good-bye... I was wondering if you might want to... / I wouldn't. I'll make you blue.
jokes.setup: Michael calls Rita to cancel; the narrator says 'so it's bye-bye to Rita'; Michael starts to shake her hand goodbye
jokes.punchline: Rita preemptively says she wouldn't (go out with him) — then adds 'I'll make you blue' — which sounds like 'I'll disappoint you' but she means literal blue finger paint
jokes.explanation: Another Rita double-meaning: 'I'll make you blue' sounds like a rejection ('I'll disappoint you / make you sad') but the narrator clarifies she means finger paint. The reveal is funny both for subverting the rejection and for the image of a grown woman casually mentioning finger paint in a social context. Rewatch bonus for Rita's secret.

jokes.text: Rita was just talking about the finger paint, but Michael didn't know that.
jokes.setup: Rita says 'I'll make you blue' in a context Michael misreads
jokes.punchline: The narrator's flat explanation — standard AD practice of explaining misunderstandings — is itself the joke: the explanation ('finger paint') is so mundane compared to what Michael probably feared/hoped she meant
jokes.explanation: The narrator-as-explainer is an AD signature. Here it also seeds confusion about Rita's situation — why does a teacher have finger paint on her hands at the moment she's on the phone? On rewatch, the answer is clearer and funnier.

jokes.text: Had he been Jack the Ripper, he would have soothed himself in a most unsavory way. But instead he just sat in his car and ate a whole thing of candy beans.
jokes.setup: Michael is filled with self-loathing after fumbling his goodbye with Rita
jokes.punchline: The narrator compares Michael's coping mechanism (candy beans) to Jack the Ripper's (murder) — implying both are valid emotional regulation strategies on the same continuum
jokes.explanation: A magnificent use of the narrator. The mock-serious comparison between serial murder and stress-eating candy beans is dark comedy at its most precise. 'A whole thing of candy beans' as the pathetic-but-relatable alternative to murder is perfect. The Ripper callback gives it added depth. A genuine standout moment.

jokes.text: Gob, I know you're looking for a twin for your illusion, and there's somebody I'd like to put in a plug for. / Correction: make that 4,000 plugs.
jokes.setup: Tobias offers himself as a look-alike for Gob's illusion and says he'd like to 'put in a plug'
jokes.punchline: He corrects himself — 'make that 4,000 plugs' — revealing he has had 4,000 hair plugs installed, visible on his now-absurdly plugged head
jokes.explanation: The double meaning of 'plug' (promote oneself vs. hair plugs) with the visual payoff of 4,000 hair plugs is a classic AD multi-layer joke. The self-correction structure is tight and the number '4,000' is specifically funny — not 400, not 1,000, but 4,000. High rewatch value because the visual must be absurd.

jokes.text: Where every day it rains... pennies from heaven!
jokes.setup: George Sr. describes his planned escape route through storm drains
jokes.punchline: His poetic reverie — 'where every day it rains pennies from heaven' — about the literal storm drains he'll flee through is so incongruously romantic for a fugitive's escape tunnel that it becomes absurdist comedy
jokes.explanation: George Sr.'s sincere romanticism about storm drains — 'pennies from heaven' — is a beautiful absurdist character moment. The gap between the grim reality (fleeing through sewers) and his poetic framing is large enough to generate a genuine laugh.

jokes.text: It was a... bird day...
jokes.setup: Gob's illusion is called 'Free Bird' (or 'Free Chicken'); the scene involves George Sr. planning an escape
jokes.punchline: The narrator's 'It was a bird day' — a pun on 'birthday' given that this is the day George Sr. will attempt to flee — but also playing on the bird/chicken/Free Bird motif running through the episode
jokes.explanation: A quick narrator pun connecting multiple running threads. Efficient but not a standout — the pun is a bit loose ('bird day' vs. 'birthday' doesn't have the precision of the episode's better wordplay).

jokes.text: Oh, God! / Oh, come on. / Mr. Gay, he's bleeding. Mr. Gay... / No, no, no — I was scared too, but I realized it was of being a leading man.
jokes.setup: Gob reacts in horror to Tobias's 4,000 hair plugs
jokes.punchline: Someone calls out 'Mr. Gay' — Tobias's surname — which sounds like the epithet, and Tobias interprets 'scared' not of the hair plugs but of 'being a leading man'
jokes.explanation: The 'Mr. Gay' surname gag running alongside Tobias's oblivious redirection (fear of being a leading man vs. fear of his own appearance) is good character comedy. Tobias interpreting everyone's horror as reaction to his theatrical potential is perfectly delusional.

jokes.text: Oh, I can just taste those meaty leading-man parts in my mouth.
jokes.setup: Tobias is excited about the prospect of being a leading man in Gob's illusion
jokes.punchline: 'I can just taste those meaty leading-man parts in my mouth' is another in the endless stream of Tobias double entendres — 'parts' means acting roles but is also anatomically suggestive
jokes.explanation: Another strong Tobias entendre. 'Meaty leading-man parts in my mouth' is the kind of sentence that, once heard, cannot be unheard. The word 'meaty' does heavy lifting here. Character-specific to the point of being a Tobias signature.

jokes.text: All right, the trick has changed. We want you to play me now. / Oh, but that would mean I'd have to sh— / Shave my very tender head. I see.
jokes.setup: Gob asks Tobias to shave his head (now covered in 4,000 plugs) to look like Gob
jokes.punchline: Tobias starts to respond 'I'd have to sh—' — clearly about to say 'shave' — then his self-interruption and the delivery of 'shave my very tender head' implies the censored word might have been something else, and his horror is about losing the plugs
jokes.explanation: The mid-word cut on 'sh—' creates a beat of suspense (is he going to say something else?) before the resolution. The horror of potentially shaving 4,000 freshly-installed hair plugs is good physical comedy inferred from context.

jokes.text: Okay. Well, perhaps I jumped the gun on those new head shots.
jokes.setup: Tobias might have to shave his head immediately after getting 4,000 plugs
jokes.punchline: He laments having 'jumped the gun on those new head shots' — implying he already commissioned professional headshots featuring his brand-new plugged hair
jokes.explanation: The defeated, practical acknowledgment that he wasted money on headshots for a hairline he immediately has to remove is perfectly Tobias. The understatement of 'perhaps I jumped the gun' when the plugs were just installed is beautifully timed.

jokes.text: An unlicensed Mary Poppins takes down another tourist. Is this the work of the Itsy Bitsy IRA?
jokes.setup: George Michael mentions seeing the Poppins incident on the news
jokes.punchline: The news framing — 'unlicensed Mary Poppins takes down another tourist' (implying it's a recurring problem) and 'Is this the work of the Itsy Bitsy IRA?' — escalates Wee Britain's street violence into a terrorist subplot
jokes.explanation: 'Itsy Bitsy IRA' is one of the great joke constructions of this episode. It takes the Wee Britain absurdism and escalates it to a dark political satire level — a tiny (Itsy Bitsy) Irish Republican Army operating inside a fake British enclave in California, blamed for Mary Poppins street violence. The callback to the earlier Poppins incident pays off with an exponentially bigger joke. This is exceptional AD writing.

jokes.text: An unlicensed Mary Poppins takes down another tourist. Is this the work of the Itsy Bitsy IRA?
jokes.setup: George Michael mentions the Poppins incident was on the news
jokes.punchline: The news headline: 'An unlicensed Mary Poppins takes down another tourist. Is this the work of the Itsy Bitsy IRA?' — treating a street performer accident as potential IRA terrorism, with the diminutive name 'Itsy Bitsy IRA'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's standout jokes. 'The Itsy Bitsy IRA' is an astonishing piece of comedy writing — it's a diminutive nickname for a terrorist organization that implies they're specifically small, perhaps specializing in small-scale British-themed disruptions. The news headline treating a street performer as potential IRA activity is perfect Wee Britain world-building comedy taken to its logical absurdist extreme. The name alone ('Itsy Bitsy') is quotable independently. This is close to an 8.5 on craft — the concept is genuinely original and perfectly constructed.

jokes.text: We lost Andy. / What are you talking about? / He didn't like his trailer. / He thought we were making fun of him. / No one was making fun of Andy Griffith. I can't emphasize that enough.
jokes.setup: George Sr. and Michael had agreed to get Andy Griffith for the courthouse scene
jokes.punchline: Andy Griffith pulled out because 'he didn't like his trailer' and 'thought we were making fun of him' — implying Andy Griffith has a serious enough practice as a fake legal decoration that he has trailer requirements; Michael's insistence that 'no one was making fun of Andy Griffith' suggests they really were
jokes.explanation: Strong callback to the Andy Griffith premise. The fact that Andy Griffith has trailer requirements for his 'legal decoration' work is funny; 'He thought we were making fun of him' is funnier; but 'No one was making fun of Andy Griffith. I can't emphasize that enough.' is the best part — the over-protestation suggests the opposite. Classic AD comic protesteth-too-much.

jokes.text: We lost Andy. / What are you talking about? / He didn't like his trailer. / He thought we were making fun of him. / No one was making fun of Andy Griffith. I can't emphasize that enough.
jokes.setup: George Sr. reveals Andy Griffith dropped out of their courthouse plan
jokes.punchline: The reason is absurd (trailer complaint + thought they were mocking him), and Michael's earnest 'I can't emphasize that enough' — defending Andy Griffith's dignity — implies the plan was so disrespectful that even Michael can see why Griffith took offense
jokes.explanation: The callback to the Griffith plan ends with a dignity-focused punchline. Michael's insistence that 'no one was making fun of Andy Griffith, I can't emphasize that enough' is funny because the entire plan WAS absurdly disrespectful to Griffith while being genuine. The comedy is in Michael's blind spot.

jokes.text: A lot of people are probably here for the free chicken.
jokes.setup: Gob's renamed magic show ('Free Chicken') has attracted a crowd
jokes.punchline: George Sr. thinks people came for actual free chicken — the word 'chicken' having shed its metaphorical (cowardice) meaning and become literal
jokes.explanation: The running chicken gag intersects with the magic show plot. The name 'Free Chicken' — chosen as a cowardice-themed rebrand — now attracts literal chicken-seekers. A nice structural joke.

jokes.text: Gather round, humans...
jokes.setup: Gob begins his courtroom magic show with a theatrical address to the audience
jokes.punchline: 'Gather round, humans' — an alien/android phrasing that inadvertently suggests Gob doesn't think of himself as human
jokes.explanation: Two words that encapsulate Gob's detachment from ordinary human experience. 'Gather round, humans' as a stage address is the most Gob sentence possible — theatrical, condescending, and slightly wrong. Nearly perfect economy.

jokes.text: I've gotta start labeling these suits.
jokes.setup: Gob is apparently wearing the wrong suit for the trick
jokes.punchline: 'I've gotta start labeling these suits' implies Gob owns multiple nearly-identical suits that he routinely confuses — the visual comedy of a grown man in the wrong themed suit is inferred
jokes.explanation: A perfectly efficient character joke. Gob having so many specialized suits that he can't keep them straight is character-defining absurdism delivered in nine words.

jokes.text: Steve Holt, what are you doing here? / I just came to see the magic show. I didn't know it was you. / Do you like magic? / No. / I love it.
jokes.setup: Steve Holt arrives at Gob's magic show; Gob is alarmed; Steve says he didn't know it was Gob's show
jokes.punchline: Gob asks 'do you like magic?' presumably looking for common ground with his son — Steve says 'no' — and Gob immediately replies 'I love it,' pivoting to a non-conversation
jokes.explanation: Near-perfect character comedy. Gob tries to connect with his son and in the space of two lines completely fails — asking a question and then not listening to the answer, pivoting to talking about himself. 'Do you like magic? / No. / I love it.' is a masterclass in three lines. The structure is airtight and completely character-specific.

jokes.text: And at that moment, Gob chose to show his own son what he was capable of. / I've decided I will be performing the feat... myself. / Wait. We're back to hair?
jokes.setup: Gob has an emotional breakthrough with Steve Holt and decides to do the illusion himself instead of using a substitute
jokes.punchline: Tobias's 'Wait. We're back to hair?' — his only concern is whether Gob will now need him to have hair again (after shaving), treating the emotional father-son moment as a hair management scheduling problem
jokes.explanation: Excellent reaction beat. The emotional climax of Gob choosing to perform for his son is completely deflated by Tobias's logistics question. 'We're back to hair?' as a question treats the hair situation as a binary state machine that Gob keeps toggling. Perfect Tobias delivery — completely self-involved in the middle of someone else's emotional moment.

jokes.text: I've decided I will be performing the feat myself. / Wait, we're back to hair?
jokes.setup: Gob announces he'll perform the feat himself (as opposed to having a look-alike)
jokes.punchline: Tobias hears 'feat' as 'feat' but also possibly as a hair treatment — given the context of all the hair discussion, he asks 'we're back to hair?'
jokes.explanation: A decent wordplay beat that plays on the hair-obsessed context of the scene. 'Feat/feat (hair treatment)' is a slight stretch but earns its laugh in context.

jokes.text: Boy. Did I catch a break. [Tobias having found it too painful to part with his new hair, so he opted for a bald cap]
jokes.setup: Tobias couldn't bear to shave his 4,000 plugs, so he wears a bald cap over them
jokes.punchline: The narrator delivers 'boy, did I catch a break' in Tobias's voice/perspective — Tobias wearing a bald cap over 4,000 hair plugs is presented as a lucky outcome
jokes.explanation: The absurdist visual of a bald cap worn over 4,000 hair plugs — and Tobias's genuine relief about this — is excellent physical comedy inferred from context. The narrator's adoption of Tobias's POV ('boy, did I catch a break') is a clever formal choice.

jokes.text: This chicken... shall be wrongly cooped.
jokes.setup: Gob introduces the next part of his illusion
jokes.punchline: 'This chicken shall be wrongly cooped' — a pun on 'cooped up' (confined/imprisoned) referencing both the cage in the illusion AND George Sr.'s legal situation, while also calling back to the chicken running gag
jokes.explanation: A densely layered pun for seven words. 'Wrongly cooped' connects the chicken motif to the imprisonment motif and the magic cage in one phrase. 'Shall be' gives it the proper Gob theatrical register. Strong wordplay that rewards the attentive viewer.

jokes.text: I fooled his own son. I am a leading man.
jokes.setup: The magic trick has concluded; Tobias (as Gob's double) has apparently deceived Steve Holt into thinking Tobias is Gob
jokes.punchline: Tobias triumphantly declares 'I fooled his own son. I am a leading man.' — treating the deception of a son who doesn't know his father as an acting achievement
jokes.explanation: The sad irony of Tobias considering this his greatest acting achievement — deceiving an abandoned son — while Gob has just abandoned him again, is dark and perfectly character-specific. 'I am a leading man' as the conclusion of this sequence is a great line.

jokes.text: I'm sorry, Steve. I need to be a father. [Gob running through storm drains]
jokes.setup: Gob has used the trick to escape the courthouse and is fleeing through storm drains
jokes.punchline: He says 'I need to be a father' while literally running away from his son — the statement is sincere but the action is the exact opposite
jokes.explanation: The juxtaposition of 'I need to be a father' said while running away from your child through drainage infrastructure is a perfect Gob moment. His self-awareness and self-deception occupy the same sentence. Strong character irony.

jokes.text: [George Sr.'s escape attempt is foiled; someone holds up a chicken to keep him out of the kitchen] Keeps him out of the kitchen.
jokes.setup: George Sr. tries to escape during the commotion
jokes.punchline: Someone uses a literal chicken to keep George Sr. confined — 'keeps him out of the kitchen' — playing on his fear of something and the running chicken gag
jokes.explanation: The chicken gag's final payoff in this episode — a literal chicken used as an escape deterrent. The matter-of-fact delivery of 'keeps him out of the kitchen' treats this as a perfectly normal solution. Good world-building absurdism.

jokes.text: We plead... not guilty. / Order in the court! Who let a flashbulb camera in?
jokes.setup: Michael had committed to pleading guilty; the judge asks for the plea
jokes.punchline: Michael says 'not guilty' — a reversal — causing chaos; the judge's bizarre complaint about 'a flashbulb camera' is a non-sequitur that makes the chaos feel period-wrong
jokes.explanation: A satisfying plot reversal moment. The 'flashbulb camera' complaint is an odd specific detail that gets a chuckle from its anachronistic feel. Not a strong joke structurally but the plea reversal lands as a moment.

jokes.text: how do you plead? / We plead... not guilty.
jokes.setup: Michael had committed to pleading guilty; the entire episode has been about whether to plead guilty
jokes.punchline: Michael pleads not guilty — a reversal based on his Rita-inspired courage, but the comedy is in the anti-climactic way the long episode debate is resolved
jokes.explanation: The payoff of the episode's central conflict. The drama of the 'not guilty' declaration is undercut by the preceding chaos (Gob escaped, Andy Griffith pulled out, Harry Hamlin is there). Functional narrative comedy beat more than a joke in itself.

jokes.text: It's Harry Hamlin. / I love my trailer.
jokes.setup: George panics that the prosecution has 'that stud from L.A. Law' — Harry Hamlin
jokes.punchline: Harry Hamlin (appearing as himself) immediately announces 'I love my trailer' — a pointed joke given that Andy Griffith just quit over trailer complaints
jokes.explanation: Harry Hamlin's cameo is perfectly deployed. His first line — 'I love my trailer' — is a direct callback to the Andy Griffith trailer complaint, distinguishing himself from Griffith's diva behavior while being equally absurd for a supposedly threatening opponent. Three words of dialogue do enormous work.

jokes.text: They don't have a prayer. / Send the check to my accountant.
jokes.setup: Michael pleads not guilty, triggering courtroom chaos
jokes.punchline: Harry Hamlin immediately declares they don't have a prayer — as a legal decoration, not an actual lawyer — and then gives billing instructions, treating his presence as a completed professional engagement
jokes.explanation: Harry Hamlin's deadpan confidence ('They don't have a prayer') from someone who is definitively not a lawyer is great. The immediate pivot to billing ('send the check to my accountant') establishes the full professional relationship — he has opinions AND an accountant for this work.

jokes.text: They don't have a [bleep] prayer. Send the check to my accountant.
jokes.setup: Harry Hamlin is apparently the prosecution's celebrity-lawyer figure
jokes.punchline: He immediately brags about how easy it'll be and redirects to his fee — treating the case as a financial transaction, not a legal matter
jokes.explanation: Good extension of Harry Hamlin's celebrity-as-legal-weapon bit. His confidence and fee-first mentality mirror the Griffith plan from the defense side, giving it satisfying structural symmetry.

jokes.text: That's Rita? / Yeah. / Wow. She is way out of his league.
jokes.setup: Michael points out Rita has come to the courthouse
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s immediate assessment — 'she is way out of his league' — delivered with complete directness to Michael's face
jokes.explanation: George Sr.'s blunt assessment to his son's face is good character-specific cruelty. Gets a rewatch bonus when you understand what Rita's 'league' actually is — she's not conventionally out of Michael's reach, just differently so.

jokes.text: And then, just to prove to himself he wasn't a coward, he went one step further. [Michael kisses Rita]
jokes.setup: Michael has asked for Rita's number and she's agreed
jokes.punchline: The narrator says Michael 'went one step further to prove he wasn't a coward' — the kiss, presented as bravery, is later revealed to be uncomfortable in light of Rita's secret
jokes.explanation: A rewatch-coded moment. On first watch, a sweet romantic beat. On rewatch, knowing Rita's MRF secret, the narrator's commentary takes on a darker irony. The episode deliberately seeds these moments.

jokes.text: For you see, Rita had a secret of her own.
jokes.setup: The episode ends with Michael and Rita's romantic moment
jokes.punchline: The narrator closes with 'For you see, Rita had a secret of her own' — a cliffhanger that retroactively recontextualizes every Rita joke in the episode
jokes.explanation: The closing narrator line is masterfully structured for rewatch. It promises a secret while retroactively making all the Rita 'odd' moments (crayons, nap time, blue finger paint, 'my whole face is British') funnier. AD's long-game comedy at its best — the episode is a setup for jokes that land fully only on subsequent viewings.

tmdb_overview: Investigating his father's claim that a British group set him up, Michael goes to "Wee Britain" and is smitten with a beautiful woman named Rita. Lindsay attempts to seduce the new family attorney, and Gob goes to extremes to avoid his newly discovered son.
insight: British spy parody sustains 2.27 jokes per minute across 61 gags with minimal setup lag.
